content.description:
# Hob Language Design Direction

## The Goal

Hob should be the obvious choice for any user-programming within Hobson. There should be no reason to choose JS except for familiarity. Hob — and the way it integrates with Hobson — should be better than JS for most things, and as good for the rest.

This is not "Hob is a nice option" or "Hob works for simple views." It's a stronger claim: a Hobson user who knows both JS and Hob should reach for Hob every time, because Hob understands Hobson in ways that JS never can.

## Why Hob can be better than JS

Hob's advantage is not that it's a better general-purpose language — it isn't, and doesn't need to be. Its advantage is that it's a **domain-native** language. Hobson concepts are Hob concepts:

- **Items are first-class.** `@my-note` is a reference to an item, not a string constant you look up. `get-field`, `set-field!`, `navigate!` are language-level operations, not API calls.
- **Code is data.** With JSON AST as canonical representation (see [proposal](c0c0c0c0-0051-0000-0000-000000000000)), Hob code is stored in the same format as everything else in Hobson. It's queryable, transformable, and inspectable with the same tools as any item property.
- **The system understands your code.** Because Hob code runs through an evaluator (not `new Function()`), the system can sandbox it, track its dependencies, inspect its state, and assist meaningfully. JS is opaque; Hob is transparent.
- **Macros extend the language.** Users can define new syntactic patterns that operate on code structure. This is impossible in JS without a build system.
- **Structural editing.** Enabled by code-as-data. Edit code structure directly, no syntax errors possible. The editor understands what you're editing because the code is structured data, not text.

## Design principles

### `invoke` is the escape hatch, not the goal

`invoke` provides general JS interop — call any JS function, access any API. It's necessary but should feel like reaching for a screwdriver when the toolbox is right there. If a Hob view is mostly `invoke` calls, that's a signal that Hob is missing a primitive, not that the view is inherently JS-native.

### Domain-specific ops are the ergonomic layer

Every pattern that recurs across Hobson programming should eventually have a Hob-native expression. The progression:

1. First occurrence: use `invoke` (prove the pattern is needed)
2. Recurrence: identify the awkward `invoke` pattern
3. Promotion: add a Hob primitive that expresses the concept directly

This is how `get-field`, `set-field!`, `navigate!`, `get-item`, etc. were born. The same process continues for interactive/spatial concepts.

### Primitives should be better, not just equivalent

When adding a new capability, don't just match what JS can do — make it better:

- **Persistent view state** should be inspectable and auto-cleaned-up, not just mutable variables
- **Sub-item rendering** should be declarative with automatic lifecycle management, not manual mount/unmount
- **Event binding** should be lifecycle-aware with automatic cleanup, not manual bookkeeping
- **Reactivity** (eventually) should be language-level dependency tracking, not framework boilerplate

### Code-as-data is the foundation

The [JSON AST proposal](item://c0c0c0c0-0051-0000-0000-000000000000) is not an optimization — it's the foundation for everything that makes Hob categorically better than JS. Queryable code, structural editing, safe macro expansion, and deep tooling all depend on code being stored as structured data.

## Current state

See: [Hob View Conversion Tracker](item://149546fb-4cf0-4a28-a79b-7cf313286bdf)

**What works well:**
- 19 views converted to Hob, including the spatial-canvas-view (most complex view in the system — 5 JS libraries, nested render-item, selector-filtered reactivity)
- JSON AST is the canonical code representation — all Hob items stored as compact JSON, evaluator dispatches directly on JSON types
- Persistent view state via atoms + get-view-config/update-view-config!
- Sub-item rendering with options map (view, sibling-container, on-cycle, decorator, navigate-to)
- Document-level events with automatic cleanup (on-document!, on-cleanup!)
- Selector-filtered reactivity via `(get-item id :select fn)` — views declare what slice of data they care about, re-renders fire only when that slice changes
- Selector pins — explicit selectors can't be widened by later bare get-item calls
- Morphdom handler retention — old event handlers kept (correct DOM refs) instead of transferred from discarded tree
- Domain ops cover the common cases for item-centric programming
- Macro system works and is used in practice
- Structural editor phases 1–6 complete

**What's next:**
- More view conversions (test-suite, hobson-instance, type-workshop are ready now)
- Structural editor phase 7 (copy/paste, comment support, context-sensitive hints)
- Reactive transclusion (static transclusion works; making it reactive/editable is the next leap)
- Making Hob the default language for new items

## Decision heuristic

When evaluating any change to Hob, ask: *does this make Hob more obviously the right choice for Hobson programming?*

- Adding a primitive? It should make the Hob version better than the JS version, not just possible.
- Designing an API? Express Hobson concepts directly, don't just wrap JS APIs.
- Building tooling? Leverage code-as-data — do things JS tooling can't.
- Converting a view? If it's mostly `invoke`, pause and build the missing primitive first.
